inconvenient.definition: Causing trouble or difficulty for others.
inconvenient.example: The child's behaviour was inconvenient but not 'bad.'

strain.definition: A feeling of stress or difficulty from too much pressure.
strain.example: I find it a strain to behave well when nobody understands me.

dashed.example: She felt stressed when her plans were dashed.

triggers.definition: Things that cause a particular reaction or behaviour.
triggers.example: Make a note of what your child's triggers are.

articulate.definition: To express thoughts or feelings clearly in words.
articulate.example: It's hard for children to articulate why they feel upset.

predominant.definition: The strongest or most noticeable feeling or thing.
predominant.example: When we are upset, that feeling is so predominant it's hard to talk.

nursery.definition: A place where young children are cared for during the day.
nursery.example: She picked up her daughter from nursery.

corridor.definition: A long, narrow passage inside a building.
corridor.example: The child ran up and down the corridor screaming.

naughty.definition: Behaving badly, especially used for children.
naughty.example: Parents often call their children naughty when they are just upset.

embarrassed.definition: Feeling uncomfortable because other people are watching.
embarrassed.example: She felt embarrassed in front of the other parents.

effective.definition: Working well and producing the result you want.
effective.example: Threatening punishment is not effective with young children.

pudding.definition: A sweet food eaten after the main meal; dessert.
pudding.example: She threatened no pudding if the child didn't behave.

buggy.definition: A small folding chair with wheels for pushing a child.
buggy.example: The child wouldn't get in the buggy.

gloves.definition: Coverings for your hands with separate parts for each finger.
gloves.example: She refused to put on her gloves in the cold.

chemist.definition: A shop that sells medicine and health products (British English).
chemist.example: They stopped at the chemist's on the way home.

shelves.definition: Flat boards fixed to a wall for holding things.
shelves.example: The child kept pulling things off the shelves.

counter.definition: A long, flat surface in a shop where you pay.
counter.example: At the counter, the child started screaming.

wrestling.definition: Struggling and fighting physically with someone.
wrestling.example: Getting the child in the buggy turned into wrestling.

despairing.definition: Feeling that there is no hope.
despairing.example: She felt despairing after losing her shopping bag.

porch.definition: A covered area at the entrance of a building.
porch.example: She left her shopping bag in the nursery porch.

crap.example: She was afraid of looking like a crap parent.

sobbing.example: She was standing with her back to the child, sobbing.

literally.definition: Used to emphasize that something is exactly true.
literally.example: I was literally standing there sobbing.

mortified.example: I'd feel mortified if people saw my child having a tantrum.

witnessing.example: She felt terrible about other parents witnessing the tantrum.

cooperation.example: She wished people could witness love and cooperation instead.

issued.example: She felt terrible that she'd issued a threat.

threat.definition: A warning that something bad will happen.
threat.example: The threat of no pudding made no difference.

uneasiness.definition: A feeling of mild worry or discomfort.
uneasiness.example: The child had an underlying feeling of uneasiness.

underlying.definition: Hidden below the surface; the real cause.
underlying.example: The underlying feeling was worry about her mother being late.

complicated.definition: Having many parts that are difficult to understand.
complicated.example: The child was playing a complicated game when her mother arrived.

insisted.definition: Said firmly that something must happen.
insisted.example: Her mother insisted she put on her coat right away.

automatic.example: Saying 'No' was an automatic reaction to sudden change.

distracted.definition: Unable to pay attention because your mind is elsewhere.
distracted.example: The mother was so distracted that the child felt alone.

scared.example: The child felt scared when she couldn't connect with her mother.

shifting gears.definition: Changing from one activity or mood to another.
shifting gears.example: Young children need more time when shifting gears.

neural.definition: Related to the nerves and brain.
neural.example: Those neural pathways will develop in time.

pathways.definition: Routes or connections, especially in the brain.
pathways.example: The brain builds neural pathways through experience.

identified.definition: Recognized or figured out what something is.
identified.example: If you identify the child's difficulty, you can help them.

admonishing.definition: Telling someone firmly that they did something wrong.
admonishing.example: You spend too much time admonishing instead of including the child.

superficially.example: Superficially, you care more about what others think.

on board.example: Soon she will learn to take your feelings on board too.

pre-empting.definition: Doing something before a problem happens to prevent it.
pre-empting.example: Pre-empting trouble saves time in the long run.

verbalize.definition: To put feelings or thoughts into words.
verbalize.example: Verbalize your child's feelings so they can learn to do it too.

predict.definition: To say what you think will happen in the future.
predict.example: Try to predict situations that your child will find hard.

tricky.definition: Difficult to deal with or do.
tricky.example: Think ahead about tricky new situations.

mindset.definition: A way of thinking about things.
mindset.example: Writing from the child's view helps you get into their mindset.

calmer.example: Understanding your child leads to a calmer time for both of you.
